elements.content: Good typography is invisible. Readers should never notice the grid, the rhythm, or the measure — they should only feel the ease with which meaning reaches them. This document exercises the fundamentals of that craft: paragraph flow, inline styling, alignment, borders, and clean pagination.

children.content: through contrast, and rhythm through repetition — all within a single flowing line.

elements.content: The measure — the width of a text column — is among the oldest constraints in type setting. Twelve to fifteen words per line is a longstanding ideal: narrow enough that the eye returns to the left margin without effort, wide enough that each line carries a full thought. This paragraph is held at the standard LETTER margin to demonstrate stable wrap positions and consistent baseline spacing.
Left-aligned — the default for most running text.
Centred — used for titles, captions, and display lines.
Right-aligned — folios, credits, and pulled-out data.

The border-left treatment derives from manuscript annotation practice: marginalia drawn with a vertical rule to distinguish commentary from the main text. All four borders are active here, padding is asymmetric, and the rule widths differ — a deliberate test of boundary geometry.

Long-form documents span many pages. The content that follows begins on a fresh page to confirm that break-before behaviour is handled cleanly — margins reset, baselines re-anchor, and flow continues as if the first page never ended.
Page two. Paragraph flow, alignment, and line-height remain identical to page one. There are no special cases here — pagination is transparent to the typographic engine.
A closing line confirms that content after the break renders in the correct position and with no residual state from the previous page.
